petitioner_background: The petitioner sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. The petitioner appears to be involved in winemaking, specifically associated with the 2006 Finger Lakes Dry Riesling Tierce wine project, which was a collaboration developed in 2006. The original petition was filed on May 24, 2007.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed both the motion to reopen and the motion to reconsider. The motion to reopen was dismissed because the evidence submitted (articles and awards from 2009) all related to events occurring after the May 24, 2007 filing of the petition, and eligibility must be established at the time of filing. Although counsel argued the evidence related to the 2006 Tierce wine project created before filing, the AAO held it could not consider facts that came into being only subsequent to the filing of the petition. The motion to reconsider was dismissed because counsel merely reiterated arguments from the original appeal and failed to support the motion with any precedent decisions or other evidence establishing that the prior decision was based on an incorrect application of law or USCIS policy. The previous AAO decision of June 16, 2009 was affirmed and the petition remains denied.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Regl. Commr. 1971)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: This decision concerns a motion to reopen and motion to reconsider following an earlier AAO dismissal. The motion to reopen failed because the submitted evidence (articles and awards from 2009) post-dated the May 24, 2007 petition filing and therefore did not constitute 'new' facts under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(2), as eligibility must be established at the time of filing. The motion to reconsider failed because counsel reiterated arguments from the original appeal without citing precedent decisions or showing an incorrect application of law or USCIS policy, failing to meet the requirements of 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(3).
